title: Rice and peas
subtitle: Jamaican kidney beans, coconut, scallion, and thyme.
excerpt: Long-grain rice simmered with kidney beans (the "peas"), coconut milk, scallion, thyme, garlic, and a single Scotch bonnet. The Sunday-dinner companion to curry goat, jerk chicken, and oxtail across the Caribbean.
sourceNotes: Jamaican household tradition with antecedents in West African rice-and-bean cookery brought across in the eighteenth century. The use of red kidney beans as "peas" is the island convention.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well portioned for up to 2 months. Defrost overnight and reheat in a covered pan with 2 tablespoons of water.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Can be cooked an hour ahead, kept covered off the heat, and reheated. Rest is built into the dish.

content.text: Long-grain rice cooked in coconut milk with red kidney beans, a couple of stalks of spring onion (called "scallion" on the island), a few sprigs of thyme, four cloves of garlic, and a whole Scotch bonnet. The bonnet sits in the pot through the cook, lifted out before serving. The rice picks up colour from the beans and perfume from the chilli without taking on serious heat.
Tinned kidney beans are the household shortcut and work perfectly. Dried beans soaked overnight and pre-cooked for an hour give a slightly nuttier result if there's time.

items.prepNote: rinsed until the water runs clear
whole, lightly crushed with the side of a knife
whole, pricked once with a knife tip

content.text: A heavy lidded saucepan large enough to hold double the volume of dry rice and liquid combined. The lid needs to fit snugly.

Tip {{kidney-beans}} of kidney beans into the saucepan with {{coconut-milk}} of coconut milk, {{water}} of water, {{garlic}} of crushed garlic, {{spring-onion}} of crushed spring onion, {{thyme}} of thyme sprigs, the whole pricked {{scotch-bonnet}} of Scotch bonnet, {{allspice}} of allspice, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Bring to a simmer and cook 5 minutes.
Stir in {{long-grain-rice}} of rinsed rice. Bring back to a simmer. Cover, drop the heat to its lowest, and cook for 25 minutes without lifting the lid. The rice steams in its own moisture.

Take off the heat and rest, sealed, 10 minutes. Lift out the Scotch bonnet, garlic skins, thyme stalks, and spring-onion pieces. Fluff with a fork. Taste and adjust salt. Serve as a side with curry goat, jerk chicken, oxtail, or any Caribbean main.

items.cause: Too much liquid, or rice not rinsed before cooking.
items.fix: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear before cooking. The ratio is 400 g rice to 700 ml total liquid (400 ml coconut + 300 ml water).

items.symptom: Rice dry and grainy on top
items.cause: Lid lifted during cooking, or heat too high.
items.fix: Don't lift the lid until the 25-minute timer goes. Use the lowest heat on the smallest burner; a heat-diffuser plate helps on a gas hob.

items.symptom: Beans burst and gravy turned mushy
items.cause: Beans cooked too long before the rice went in.
items.fix: Five minutes is enough to flavour the coconut base. Rinse tinned beans well to remove the brine that softens them further.

items.symptom: Scotch bonnet split and the rice turned painfully hot
items.cause: Bonnet over-pricked or burst in the pot.
items.fix: Prick the bonnet once, lightly, with a sharp knife tip. Lift out as soon as the cook is finished.

content.text: Rice and peas is the Sunday side of Jamaica, Trinidad, and the Caribbean diaspora. "Peas" on the island means beans; the same dish in Guyana uses gungo peas (pigeon peas) in place of kidney beans. The aroma of coconut and thyme means somebody is cooking properly. Eat with anything wet and spiced.
